Dear Research Team,

            You are collaborating to generate a new research idea based on the following Introduction:

            **Introduction**

             

1 Introduction

Humans use all facial expressions, body motions, and hand motions to express our emotions and intentions, and interact with other people and objects.
In particular, facial expressions and hand gestures are one of the most powerful channels for non-verbal communication, and hand motions are necessary to interact with diverse types of objects.
Modeling the facial expression, body motion, and hand motion altogether is extremely challenging.
Several whole-body 3D human geometry models have been introduced [21, 37, 50, 2].
Among them, SMPL-X [37] is the most widely used one, which motivated a number of 3D whole-body pose estimation methods [9, 45, 11, 32, 52, 26, 28, 4] and benchmarks [36].


To represent 3D humans beyond the minimally clothed parametric models, personalized 3D human avatars have been recently studied.
The 3D human avatar is a representation that combines 3D geometry and the appearance of a certain person, which can be animated and rendered with novel poses.
However, most of existing 3D human avatars [39, 38, 25, 8, 6, 20, 15, 19, 24, 18] modeled from a casually captured video only support body motions without facial expressions and hand motions.
Their avatars bake facial expressions and hand poses, and animating them is not possible.
A recent work [47] introduced a whole-body avatar that supports animation with facial expressions, and body and hand poses; however, it requires 3D observations, such as 3D scans or RGBD images with highly accurate SMPL-X registrations, with diverse poses and facial expressions.
Such an assumption does not hold for the majority of casually captured videos in daily life.


Figure 1: 
From (a) a monocular video from a single person, we create our (b) ExAvatar, an expressive whole-body 3D avatar, animatable with novel facial expression code, hand poses, and body poses of SMPL-X.



We present ExAvatar, an expressive whole-body 3D human avatar that can be made from a short monocular video.
ExAvatar is designed as a combination of the whole-body 3D parametric model (SMPL-X) [37] and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) [22].
It utilizes the whole-body drivability of SMPL-X and the photorealistic and efficient rendering capability of 3DGS.
After the training, it is animatable with novel facial expression code and 3D pose of SMPL-X, as shown in Fig. 1.
Despite its desired properties, modeling ExAvatar is an non-trivial task with the following two challenges: 1) a limited diversity of facial expressions and poses in the video and 2) the absence of 3D observations, such as 3D scans and RGBD videos.
The limited diversity in the video makes a drivability with novel facial expressions and poses non-trivial.
In addition, the absence of 3D observations creates ambiguity in the occluded human parts, exhibiting noticeable artifacts in novel facial expressions and poses.


To address them, we propose a novel hybrid representation of the surface mesh and 3D Gaussians in ExAvatar.
Our hybrid representation treats each 3D Gaussian as a vertex on the surface, where the vertices have pre-defined connectivity (i.e., triangle faces) between them following the mesh topology of SMPL-X.
Existing volumetric avatars [39, 38, 25, 8, 6, 20, 15, 19, 47] do not have the connectivity by the definition.
Also, previous 3DGS-based [24, 18] works consider a set of 3D Gaussian points as a point cloud without considering the connectivity between them.

Humans use all facial expressions, body motions, and hand motions to express our emotions and intentions, and interact with other people and objects.
In particular, facial expressions and hand gestures are one of the most powerful channels for non-verbal communication, and hand motions are necessary to interact with diverse types of objects.
Modeling the facial expression, body motion, and hand motion altogether is extremely challenging.
Several whole-body 3D human geometry models have been introduced [21, 37, 50, 2].
Among them, SMPL-X [37] is the most widely used one, which motivated a number of 3D whole-body pose estimation methods [9, 45, 11, 32, 52, 26, 28, 4] and benchmarks [36].


To represent 3D humans beyond the minimally clothed parametric models, personalized 3D human avatars have been recently studied.
The 3D human avatar is a representation that combines 3D geometry and the appearance of a certain person, which can be animated and rendered with novel poses.
However, most of existing 3D human avatars [39, 38, 25, 8, 6, 20, 15, 19, 24, 18] modeled from a casually captured video only support body motions without facial expressions and hand motions.
Their avatars bake facial expressions and hand poses, and animating them is not possible.
A recent work [47] introduced a whole-body avatar that supports animation with facial expressions, and body and hand poses; however, it requires 3D observations, such as 3D scans or RGBD images with highly accurate SMPL-X registrations, with diverse poses and facial expressions.
Such an assumption does not hold for the majority of casually captured videos in daily life.

From (a) a monocular video from a single person, we create our (b) ExAvatar, an expressive whole-body 3D avatar, animatable with novel facial expression code, hand poses, and body poses of SMPL-X.



We present ExAvatar, an expressive whole-body 3D human avatar that can be made from a short monocular video.
ExAvatar is designed as a combination of the whole-body 3D parametric model (SMPL-X) [37] and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) [22].
It utilizes the whole-body drivability of SMPL-X and the photorealistic and efficient rendering capability of 3DGS.
After the training, it is animatable with novel facial expression code and 3D pose of SMPL-X, as shown in Fig. 1.
Despite its desired properties, modeling ExAvatar is an non-trivial task with the following two challenges: 1) a limited diversity of facial expressions and poses in the video and 2) the absence of 3D observations, such as 3D scans and RGBD videos.
The limited diversity in the video makes a drivability with novel facial expressions and poses non-trivial.
In addition, the absence of 3D observations creates ambiguity in the occluded human parts, exhibiting noticeable artifacts in novel facial expressions and poses.

- **Lack of 3D Observations:** The absence of 3D data leads to ambiguity in occluded parts, causing artifacts in animations.
- **Hybrid Representation:** Existing volumetric avatars lack connectivity, and previous 3DGS-based works treat Gaussians as a point cloud without connectivity.

#### 3. Methodology
**Data Augmentation:**
- Use advanced data augmentation techniques to artificially increase the diversity of facial expressions and poses in the video. This can include techniques like pose synthesis, expression blending, and motion transfer.

**Depth Estimation:**
- Develop methods to estimate depth from monocular video, reducing ambiguity in occluded parts. Techniques such as monocular depth estimation using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can be employed.

**Enhanced Connectivity Between 3D Gaussians:**
- Improve the connectivity between 3D Gaussians to better represent the surface mesh. This can be achieved by introducing a connectivity graph that links neighboring Gaussians, enhancing the hybrid representation.

**Datasets and Metrics:**
- Use a combination of synthetic and real-world datasets for evaluation. Metrics such as visual quality, animation fidelity, and user satisfaction will be used to assess the proposed approach.

**Steps and Procedures:**
1. **Data Collection:** Gather a diverse dataset of short monocular videos of human subjects.
2. **Data Augmentation:** Apply advanced data augmentation techniques to increase the diversity of facial expressions and poses.
3. **Depth Estimation:** Implement monocular depth estimation methods to handle occlusions.
4. **Hybrid Representation Enhancement:** Develop a connectivity graph to link neighboring Gaussians, enhancing the hybrid representation.
5. **Model Training:** Train the model using the augmented and depth-estimated data.
6. **Evaluation:** Evaluate the model using the defined metrics and datasets.

#### 4. Expected Results
**Outcomes:**
- A more expressive and realistic avatar that can be animated with novel facial expressions, hand poses, and body poses from a short monocular video.
- Improved handling of occlusions and reduced artifacts in animations.
- Enhanced connectivity between 3D Gaussians, leading to a better hybrid representation.

**Improvements:**
- The proposed approach will address the challenges of limited diversity and lack of 3D observations, providing a more robust framework for handling occlusions and practical improvements by enabling the creation of more expressive and realistic avatars.

#### 5. Impact and Applications
**Practical Applications:**
- **Virtual Reality (VR):** Enable the creation of more realistic and expressive avatars for VR applications, enhancing user immersion.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Improve the realism of avatars in AR applications, making interactions more natural.
- **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI):** Enhance the interaction capabilities of avatars in HCI applications, leading to better user experiences.
- **Entertainment:** Create more expressive and realistic avatars for use in movies, video games, and other entertainment media.
- **Education:** Develop avatars for educational purposes, making learning more engaging and interactive.
- **Healthcare:** Use avatars for telemedicine, patient care, and rehabilitation, improving the quality of care.

**Broader Implications:**
- The research will advance the field of 3D human modeling and animation, leading to practical applications in various industries.
- It will contribute to the development of more realistic and expressive avatars, enhancing user experiences in VR, AR, and HCI applications.

#### 6. Conclusion
**Summary:**
The proposed research aims to create expressive whole-body 3D human avatars from short monocular videos by addressing the challenges of limited diversity and lack of 3D observations. The methodology includes advanced data augmentation, depth estimation, and enhanced connectivity between 3D Gaussians. The expected outcomes are a more expressive and realistic avatar that can be animated with novel facial expressions, hand poses, and body poses from a short monocular video.

**Importance:**
The research is important as it will enable the creation of more realistic and expressive avatars for various applications, advancing the field of 3D human modeling and animation.

#### 7. References
- [1] SMPL-X: A Model for 3D Human Pose Estimation.
- [2] 3D Gaussian Splatting: Photorealistic and Efficient Rendering.
- [3] ExAvatar: Combining SMPL-X and 3DGS for Animatable Avatars.
- [4] Monocular Depth Estimation Using CNNs.
- [5] Advanced Data Augmentation Techniques for Facial Expressions and Poses.
- [6] Connectivity Graphs for 3D Gaussian Representations.

---

This detailed research proposal outlines the methodology, expected results, and potential impact of the proposed research, providing a comprehensive plan for further development and potential publication.
